name: Ogden auto shop solvents gave me headaches for months—how to document for a claim?

mainEntity.name: Ogden auto shop solvents gave me headaches for months—how to document for a claim?

acceptedAnswer.text: Keep a symptom diary, take photos of the ventilation setup, and seek specialized medical testing to prove chemical toxicity. To move beyond workers' comp into a tort claim, you'll need evidence that a third party or defective product caused the exposure.
acceptedAnswer.description:
Solvents in auto shops, such as degreasers and paints, can cause long-term neurological and respiratory issues. To document this for a "tort claim" (lawsuit), you need a consistent record of symptoms, environmental conditions, and medical evaluations. A tort claim requires proving negligence, so you must show the shop didn't provide proper ventilation (like fans or hoods) or required respirators.

Documentation checklist:
- A personal diary of every day you felt "solvent head" or dizzy.
- Air quality readings if you can get them (or photos of poor ventilation).
- Neurological testing (like a "tox screen" or nerve conduction study) by a specialist.

A "tort" (lawsuit) is only possible if someone other than your employer is at fault, or if your employer is a "non-subscriber" in states like Texas. In Utah, you would likely be limited to workers' comp unless a third party is involved. Consulting a lawyer early is the only way to ensure you don't miss the chance to gather evidence while the shop is in its current condition.
